caveats: a: O*NET is occupational rather than establishment-level evidence.
a: Vendor evidence demonstrates technical feasibility, not representative adoption or labor savings.
a: The product mix from toll ingot to molten-metal delivery materially changes task composition.
rho: No representative adoption study was found for NAICS 331314.
rho: Implementation feasibility differs sharply between sorting and closed-loop furnace control.
rho: Safety and metallurgical qualification requirements slow realization.
e: The principal mix statistic is old and drawn from one large operator.
e: Revenue mix is not identical to the share of firms meeting the lens.
e: Some buy/sell contracts have service-like customer relationships despite metal ownership.
s5: Cross-industry owner intentions are not observed metal-industry transactions.
s5: A stated succession intention may not result in a marketed or financeable company.
s5: The small population makes realized availability lumpy.

q: Capture differs between tolling and buy/sell models.
q: Competitive capacity additions could pass productivity gains to customers faster.
d5: End-market aluminum growth does not translate one-for-one into merchant secondary-smelting volume.
d5: New recycling capacity may increase total demand while reducing incumbent utilization.
d5: Automotive and industrial cycles can overwhelm structural growth over five years.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Secondary Smelting and Alloying of Aluminum

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are automotive, casting, rolling, extrusion, packaging, and other aluminum users. Revenue is earned through per-pound or per-ton toll conversion charges when the customer retains metal ownership, or through the spread between purchased scrap and sold specification alloy in buy/sell operations; energy and metal-price adjustments are often passed through contractually.
lens.excluded_activities: Primary aluminum production; scrap brokers and sorters that do not melt; captive recycling units inside large integrated producers or automotive plants; commodity traders; and businesses whose economics are principally metal-price speculation rather than processing service.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market operators that repeatedly melt, refine, blend, and alloy customer-owned aluminum scrap, dross, or purchased secondary feedstock into specified billet, ingot, sow, or molten-metal products, including toll processors and regional secondary smelters.

a.bridge: O*NET task and work-context data for metal-refining furnace operators describe the occupation central to this industry, while TOMRA's aluminum-sorting system demonstrates commercially available AI-enabled classification upstream of the furnace. These are translated into the share of total industry labor hours exposed to AI assistance or automation. The proxy may overstate near-term exposure if sorting is performed by suppliers and understate it where scheduling, chemistry, maintenance, quality records, and commercial administration are digitized.
caveats: O*NET is occupational rather than establishment-level evidence.
Vendor evidence demonstrates technical feasibility, not representative adoption or labor savings.
The product mix from toll ingot to molten-metal delivery materially changes task composition.
a.change_evidence: A task-time study from several independent secondary smelters, separated into furnace, sorting, laboratory, maintenance, logistics, sales, and administrative hours, plus measured AI cycle-time reductions by task.
a.rationale: Charging, melting, skimming, sampling, alloy adjustment, casting, maintenance, and hazardous-material handling remain physical and site-bound. AI can nevertheless improve scrap identification, blend recipes, furnace set-points, predictive maintenance, inspection, documentation, quoting, and dispatch. The base therefore places roughly one quarter of labor hours in automatable or augmentable cognitive and sensing work, not in autonomous smelting.

d5.bridge: Industry-association recycling and automotive indicators plus a downstream producer's end-market forecasts are mapped to five-year real demand for secondary-smelting services. They capture aluminum demand and recycling intensity rather than this narrow service segment; the translation may overstate firm-level demand where captive recycling or new capacity takes share and understate it if decarbonization accelerates secondary-content requirements.
caveats: End-market aluminum growth does not translate one-for-one into merchant secondary-smelting volume.
New recycling capacity may increase total demand while reducing incumbent utilization.
Automotive and industrial cycles can overwhelm structural growth over five years.
d5.change_evidence: Independent forecasts of U.S. secondary-smelter tolling volumes, utilization, scrap spreads, announced capacity, captive-versus-merchant share, and customer qualification pipelines through 2031.
d5.rationale: Secondary aluminum benefits from a large recycled share of U.S. production, major energy savings versus primary metal, very high automotive scrap recovery, and forecast growth in automotive aluminum content, can stock, and aerospace rolled products. Cyclical manufacturing volumes, scrap availability, trade policy, substitution, and substantial recycling investment temper the upside.

e.bridge: A 2016 Real Alloy presentation reported an approximately 53% tolling and 47% buy/sell mix for a large North American and European secondary-aluminum operator. That company-level revenue model is down-weighted to estimate the fraction of small U.S. establishments fitting the recurring-service lens. It may overstate the target share if large operators win more toll contracts, or understate it if small regional firms specialize in customer-owned feedstock.
caveats: The principal mix statistic is old and drawn from one large operator.
Revenue mix is not identical to the share of firms meeting the lens.
Some buy/sell contracts have service-like customer relationships despite metal ownership.
e.change_evidence: A current census or transaction database classifying independent U.S. secondary smelters by toll-processing revenue share, metal ownership, customer concentration, and firm size.
e.rationale: Toll conversion clearly exists and produces service-like fees insulated from direct metal ownership, but many secondary smelters buy scrap and sell alloy on spread economics. With only 23 estimated LMM firms and no current establishment-level breakdown, a minority base share and wide interval are appropriate.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 331314 ($515,935,497) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 331314 ($9,313,865,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $1,630,579 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 331314); class <5: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,053,182 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,187,634 -> IN $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,152,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,010,921 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,890,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,491,149 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $62,858,056 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,332,750 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $181,630,857 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $35,635,974 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $415,749,538 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $81,570,059 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 23

caveats: The range addresses AI-related obsolescence, not ordinary competitive displacement.
Large customers may vertically integrate even if the physical process remains necessary.
Environmental regulation can close assets without making the service technologically obsolete.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers replacing outsourced toll smelting with captive capacity, fully autonomous secondary-smelting lines, or process innovations that eliminate conventional remelting at commercial scale.
o.rationale: AI does not remove the need to receive physical scrap, operate high-temperature furnaces, control chemistry, manage emissions and dross, cast or deliver metal, and satisfy safety and customer qualifications. Some substitution is possible through customer insourcing, integrated recyclers, or alternative feedstock routes, but software alone cannot replace the service during the horizon.

q.bridge: Real Alloy disclosures describe per-pound or per-ton toll charges, energy pass-throughs, metal-spread buy/sell economics, local just-in-time delivery, and high customer retention. These contract features are translated into the share of AI-enabled operating value retained by the processor. The large-operator proxy may overstate bargaining power and retention for small firms, while explicit pass-throughs may understate capture of yield, uptime, and capacity gains.
caveats: Customer-retention and contract descriptions come primarily from one operator.
Capture differs between tolling and buy/sell models.
Competitive capacity additions could pass productivity gains to customers faster.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level evidence on repricing clauses and renewal cadence, paired with realized margins before and after yield, energy, quality, and uptime improvements at independent toll processors.
q.rationale: Customers can observe metal and energy economics, negotiate conversion fees, and capture savings at renewal, limiting permanent margin retention. Operators can retain part of the benefit through additional throughput, lower alloy losses, fewer rejects, reduced downtime, and differentiated reliability, especially where proximity and qualification make relationships sticky.

caveats: No representative adoption study was found for NAICS 331314.
Implementation feasibility differs sharply between sorting and closed-loop furnace control.
Safety and metallurgical qualification requirements slow realization.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level before-and-after data on deployment cost, adoption time, throughput, yield, energy intensity, downtime, quality rejects, and labor hours across multiple independent secondary smelters.
rho.rationale: Brownfield plants can add vision sorting, laboratory analytics, blend optimization, energy controls, maintenance prediction, and workflow software without replacing the furnace line. Realization is constrained by legacy controls, dirty and variable scrap streams, small engineering staffs, capital approval, emissions controls, customer qualification, and the consequences of off-spec chemistry or unsafe process changes.

s5.bridge: Gallup's 2025 survey of U.S. small-business owners provides five-year owner disposition intentions across sectors. Its sell, transfer, closure, and no-plan findings are mapped to the probability that an otherwise suitable small secondary smelter becomes actionable for acquisition. The proxy can overstate availability because environmental liabilities and capital intensity deter buyers, or understate it because aging industrial owners may be more succession-constrained than the cross-industry sample.
caveats: Cross-industry owner intentions are not observed metal-industry transactions.
A stated succession intention may not result in a marketed or financeable company.
The small population makes realized availability lumpy.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction and ownership-age dataset for independent secondary smelters, including failed processes, family transfers, closures, and environmental-liability outcomes.
s5.rationale: Owner aging creates a plausible succession channel, but an actionable industrial sale requires more than stated intent: acceptable environmental diligence, permits, equipment condition, customer retention, and financing. The base is close to the broad small-business sale/transfer signal while preserving a wide range for this thin industry.

principal_driver: Durable demand for low-energy recycled aluminum combined with measurable yield, uptime, and quality gains in qualified local processing relationships.
principal_weakness: A very small, heterogeneous acquisition pool with heavy physical capital and uncertain toll-processing incidence limits scalable deployment of the thesis.
